body:
Switzerland has deliberately enacted no omnibus crypto statute -- there is no Swiss MiCA. Instead, Switzerland regulates digital assets by applying and, where needed, amending its existing, technology-neutral financial-market law. The one dedicated legislative package in this area, the Federal Act on the Adaptation of Federal Law to Developments in Distributed Electronic Register Technology -- known as the DLT Act, adopted by the Federal Assembly on 25 September 2020 -- did not create a new crypto code; it amended roughly ten existing federal statutes, in two stages: the civil-law provisions enabling ledger-based securities took effect on 1 February 2021, and the remainder -- including the new DLT trading facility licence category and its implementing ordinance -- took effect on 1 August 2021. That single fact -- amend and apply existing law, rather than legislate a new statute -- is the throughline of every pillar board on this site for this jurisdiction, and is worth holding in mind before reading any of them.

Who regulates what

FINMA, the Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority, is Switzerland's single integrated financial-market supervisor -- a contrast worth noting against the fragmented, multi-agency picture in, for example, the United States. FINMA licenses banks, securities firms, portfolio managers, trustees, collective-investment schemes, and financial-market infrastructures, and it is FINMA that has built, through interpretive guidance and case-by-case classification rather than new rulemaking, most of the operative detail a reader will find on this site's pillar boards. Above FINMA, the Federal Council and the Federal Assembly (Parliament) legislate and set policy direction, with the State Secretariat for International Finance (SIF), part of the Federal Department of Finance, leading policy development and running public consultations such as the one on stablecoin and crypto licensing described below. The Swiss National Bank (SNB) oversees payment systems and, separately from ordinary supervision, runs Project Helvetia, its own pilot for settling tokenised-asset transactions in wholesale central bank digital currency (wCBDC) on regulated infrastructure -- a pilot the SNB itself describes as carrying no commitment to a permanent wCBDC. Below FINMA, self-regulatory organisations (SROs) recognised under the Anti-Money Laundering Act (AMLA) supervise financial intermediaries -- including most crypto businesses -- that are not otherwise prudentially supervised by FINMA directly.

What is already in force

The DLT Act's full amendments have been in force since 1 August 2021, having entered in the two stages described above. Its civil-law core -- new provisions inserted into the Code of Obligations creating "ledger-based securities," native on-chain securities with full civil-law effect and no licensing pre-clearance required to issue one -- has applied since 1 February 2021. On the supervisory side, FINMA's practice of classifying tokens as payment, utility, or asset tokens dates to its ICO guidelines of 16 February 2018, and remains the classification lens applied to every later question of licensing, custody, or dealing. FINMA has since layered further guidance onto that base: a 2019 supplement addressing "stable coins" specifically, and Guidance 06/2024 addressing the bank-default-guarantee structure many Swiss stablecoin issuers now use to avoid triggering a banking licence; Guidance 08/2023 on staking, distinguishing individually attributable ("direct") staking from pooled staking that can require a banking licence; and Guidance 01/2026 on the custody of crypto-based assets, published 12 January 2026. On market infrastructure, SIX Digital Exchange received Switzerland's first approvals for a tokenised-securities stock exchange and central securities depository on 10 September 2021 (via the ordinary FMIA route, not the DLT Act's new licence category), and the DLT Act's own "DLT trading facility" licence category was first used on 18 March 2025, when FINMA licensed BX Digital AG. AMLA, the technology-neutral anti-money-laundering statute FINMA has applied to crypto businesses since well before any of the above, continues to apply throughout: crypto businesses are financial intermediaries under AMLA and must either join an FINMA-recognised SRO or hold a licence bringing them under FINMA's direct AML supervision.

What is still evolving

The clearest live edge of Swiss digital-asset regulation is a proposed amendment to the Federal Act on Financial Institutions (FinIA), on which the Federal Department of Finance opened a public consultation on 22 October 2025 that closed on 6 February 2026. The draft would abolish the Banking Act's Article 1b "fintech licence" and replace it, within FinIA, with two new categories: "payment instrument institutions," which could issue value-stable crypto-based means of payment against segregated, redeemable backing; and "crypto-institutions," a broader category for other crypto services under licensing criteria modelled on, but lighter than, the securities-firm framework. This is the closest Switzerland has come to a dedicated crypto licence -- and it is, precisely, still structurally an amendment to an existing act, not a new statute. As of this writing, no dispatch (Botschaft) to Parliament has been confirmed adopted, and no officially stated timetable for entry into force exists; this site records only the consultation's officially stated status, not a prediction of its outcome or timing.

Two scope notes worth stating plainly. First, Switzerland is not a member state of the European Union, and the EU's Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA) does not apply there; the Swiss and EU regimes operate under separate legal systems and must never be read as interchangeable or conflated on this site. Second, Swiss official texts are authentic in German, French, and Italian; English is, on many FINMA and Confederation pages, an official courtesy translation rather than an authentic legal text. This radar cites official English-language versions where the regulator has published one, and identifies the source language explicitly where it has not.

How to use this site

The pillar boards give the current state of each of the site's seven regulatory areas for Switzerland in one place. The trajectory board lists officially announced dates and windows -- including the FinIA consultation above and the SNB's Project Helvetia timeline -- together. The document library indexes every primary-source document the watcher has observed. The glossary defines the jargon used throughout, and terms shared with other jurisdictions link to a single cross-jurisdiction entry. Every summary card cites the specific primary source it is drawn from.

AI-generated summary for general information. Not legal or regulatory advice. Always verify against the linked primary source.
